perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance w ith the laws of the Province of British Columbia , without regard to or application of choice -of-law rules or principles and the parties submit to the jurisdiction of BC courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance w ith the laws of the Province of British Columbia , without regard to or application of choice -of-law rules or principles.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and the parties submit to the jurisdiction of BC courts', the agreement attempts to waive the explicit consent to the jurisdiction of BC courts. This makes enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10 of the Court Jurisdiction Act outlines the basis for assuming jurisdiction in British Columbia. By removing the explicit submission to BC courts, the agreement creates ambiguity regarding jurisdiction, potentially contradicting the requirements for establishing jurisdiction as defined in the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly submits to the jurisdiction of BC courts, while the changed text removes this submission, creating a structural flaw that undermines enforceability. The Perturbation Explanation notes this makes enforcement uncertain. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly state the need for explicit submission to jurisdiction, the Law Explanation posits that removing this explicit submission creates ambiguity regarding jurisdiction under the Court Jurisdiction Act. The removal of the phrase weakens the agreement's enforceability within BC courts.

perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges that all of the Confidential Information is owned solely by FNHA and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, Company agrees that FNHA will have the right to obtain an immediate injunction enjoining any breach of this Agreement, as well as the rig ht to pursue any and all other rights and remedies available at law o r in equity for such a breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges that all of the Confidential Information is owned solely by FNHA and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury. Accordingly, Company agrees that FNHA will have the right to obtain an immediate injunction enjoining any breach of this Agreement, as well as the rig ht to pursue any and all other rights and remedies available at law o r in equity for such a breach.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain', the company removes acknowledgment that damages will be difficult to ascertain. This affects the ability to gain an immediate injunction by weakening the claim of irreparable harm.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia Law - Requirements for Injunctions
perturbation.law_citation: RJR-MacDonald Inc. v. Canada (Attorney General), [1994] 1 SCR 311
perturbation.law_explanation: The RJR-MacDonald case establishes a test for granting interlocutory injunctions, requiring a showing of irreparable harm if the injunction is not granted. Removing the acknowledgment that the harm may be difficult to ascertain weakens the applicant's claim that damages will be difficult to quantify, which is a part of showing irreparable harm.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Téléphone Guèvremont Inc. v. Quebec (Régie des télécommunications) - SCC Cases Mailing List RSS Feeds JSON Feeds Date modified: 2025-07-11
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the phrase 'the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain,' which directly relates to establishing irreparable harm. The Law Explanation states that the RJR-MacDonald case requires showing irreparable harm for an injunction. The Perturbation Explanation elaborates that removing this phrase weakens the claim that damages are hard to quantify, a component of proving irreparable harm. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 only mentions Téléphone Guèvremont Inc. v. Quebec, it still represents a SCC case, strengthening the pertinence of the provided legal context. This change creates a contradiction because the original text acknowledges a factor that strengthens the argument for irreparable harm, while the changed text omits this acknowledgment, thus weakening a key element needed to obtain an injunction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement does not preclude discussions of Confidential Information between the undersigned and FNHA staff or with any other persons identified b y FNHA as having undertaken this Agreement. This Agreement does not preclude discussions of Confidential Information between the undersigned and other persons , if expressly permitted by staff of FNHA and those discussions are conducted on the terms and c onditions that FNHA may direct.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not preclude discussions of Confidential Information between the undersigned and FNHA staff or with any other persons identified b y FNHA as having undertaken this Agreement. This Agreement does not preclude discussions of Confidential Information between the undersigned and other persons .
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'if expressly permitted by staff of FNHA and those discussions are conducted on the terms and conditions that FNHA may direct', removes the limitations put on the confidential information, opening it to discussions without FNHA's permission.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia Law - Breach of Confidence
perturbation.law_citation: LAC Minerals Ltd. v. International Corona Resources Ltd., [1989] 2 SCR 574
perturbation.law_explanation: The LAC Minerals case sets out the elements of a breach of confidence action, including that the information must have been communicated in confidence. By removing the explicit restrictions on discussing confidential information, the amended agreement weakens the ability to prove that the information was communicated in confidence, contradicting the established legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Hémond v. Coopérative fédérée du Québec - SCC Cases Mailing List RSS Feeds JSON Feeds Date modified: 2025-07-11
perturbation.justification: The original text requires FNHA's permission for discussions about confidential information, while the changed text removes this requirement, allowing discussions without FNHA's consent. The Law Explanation states that the LAC Minerals case requires that confidential information must have been communicated in confidence. The modified agreement weakens the ability to prove that information was communicated in confidence by removing the explicit restrictions on discussing confidential information. This falls under Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, because the organization of the contract affects its enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Company will not make copies of , disclose, discuss, publish or dissemi nate Confidential Information any third person or entity. Company will not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than responding to the RFP.
perturbation.changed_text: Company will not make copies of , disclose, discuss, publish or dissemi nate Confidential Information any third person or entity.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the statement that 'Company will not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than responding to the RFP', the user opens up the ability for the company to use it for purposes beyond responding to the RFP.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Hodgkinson v. Simms case establishes principles of fiduciary duty. While this NDA may not establish a full fiduciary relationship, it aims to protect confidential information. Removing the restriction on using the information solely for the RFP purpose weakens the implied duty to act in the best interest of FNHA regarding the information, opening the door to potential breaches of confidence or conflicts of interest. This could contradict the underlying principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Canadian Pacific Ltd. v. Matsqui Indian Band - SCC Cases Mailing List RSS Feeds JSON Feeds Date modified: 2025-07-11
perturbation.justification: The original text restricted the use of confidential information solely for responding to the RFP, while the changed text removes this restriction, opening the door for the company to use it for other purposes. This alteration potentially contradicts principles of good faith and fair dealing, as noted in the Law Explanation, by weakening the implied duty to protect FNHA's interests concerning the information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not relevant, the removal of the specific purpose clause introduces a structural flaw that weakens the enforceability of confidentiality, which is a legal contradiction according to the 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be amended except by the written agreement signed by authorized representatives of both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended by the agreement signed by representatives of both parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'written' and 'authorized' prior to representatives, it opens up to the amendment of the agreement with oral agreements. It also removes the fact that representatives needs to be authorized.
perturbation.law_citation: British Columbia Sale of Goods Act, [RSBC 1996] CHAPTER 410, s. 11
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Sale of Goods Act is not directly applicable to an NDA, it demonstrates the legal expectation for clarity and formality in contract modifications. Removing the requirement for a 'written' agreement contradicts the intent for a clear, documented agreement and introduces ambiguity about the enforceability of oral modifications. Section 11 of the Act implies changes must be clear, just like the now removed 'written' condition.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires amendments to be 'written' and signed by 'authorized' representatives. The changed text removes both of these requirements. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this opens the door for oral agreements and agreements from non-authorized representatives. The Law Explanation states that removing the requirement for a 'written' agreement contradicts the intent for a clear, documented agreement and introduces ambiguity about the enforceability of oral modifications. While the law snippet references the Sale of Goods Act, it uses Section 11 to imply changes must be clear, just like the now removed 'written' condition. The modified text weakens the clarity and enforceability of contract modifications, creating a Structural Flaw Legal Contradiction.
